_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: File I/O practice 1
questions.prompt: Which stream type reads from a file?
choices: std::ifstream
std::ofstream
std::cout
std::string
questions.explanation: An ifstream is an input file stream, while an ofstream sends output into a file.

questions.title: File I/O practice 2
choices: The body runs only after a successful read
It reads the entire file at once
It sorts the lines
It keeps the stream open forever
questions.explanation: The returned stream state becomes false when reading fails, preventing the loop from processing invalid data.

questions.title: Trace File I/O
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
accepted: One output line for each line in courses.txt.
questions.explanation: The loop processes each line only after getline succeeds, so it does not repeat stale data after the final read. The resulting output is 'One output line for each line in courses.txt.'.

questions.title: File I/O good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for File I/O?
choices: Check that opening succeeded.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Check that opening succeeded. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: File I/O practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices: Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: EOF becomes known only after a read fails. Testing getline directly prevents one extra pass with old or empty data.

questions.title: File I/O practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Relative paths start at the working directory'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.

A path such as `courses.txt` is resolved from the program?s current working directory, which may differ from the source-code folder. Print or configure the working directory when a file exists but the program cannot find it.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: A path such as `courses.txt` is resolved from the program?s current working directory, which may differ from the source-code folder. Print or configure the working directory when a file exists but the program cannot find it.

questions.title: File I/O practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for File I/O?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Process data only after a successful read.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Process data only after a successful read. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: File I/O practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for File I/O?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Know the working directory for relative paths.
questions.explanation: Know the working directory for relative paths. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: File I/O practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for File I/O?
choices: Let stream objects close files through their destructors.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Let stream objects close files through their destructors. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: File I/O practice 10
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
accepted: One output line for each line in courses.txt.
questions.explanation: The loop processes each line only after getline succeeds, so it does not repeat stale data after the final read. The output is 'One output line for each line in courses.txt.'.
